curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: simplifying ratios and ratio problems; introduction to the golden ratio
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Simplify ratios into simplest form, solve part-to-whole and scaling problems using ratio reasoning, and explore the golden ratio (approximately 1.618) as a proportional relationship seen in nature and design, linking ratio thinking to inclusive space design (SDG 11). :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: How can ratios help us compare fairly, spot patterns in proportion, and design spaces that feel inclusive and human?

knowing_world.focus: Ratios compare quantities and can be simplified by dividing by common factors. Students practise simplifying, solving part-to-whole ratio questions, and explore the golden ratio as an irrational proportion found in nature and art.
key_concepts: Simplest form by dividing by common factors
Golden ratio (φ) as an irrational ratio ≈ 1.618
Checking golden ratio via long ÷ short
activities: If the ratio of apples to oranges is 3:5, how many oranges are there in 40 pieces of fruit?
Find two lengths where (longer ÷ shorter) is approximately 1.618.

human_story.focus: Fibonacci’s sequence (1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, …) appears in natural patterns. Renaissance artists and designers (including Leonardo da Vinci) explored proportion and the golden ratio to create human-feeling designs, raising questions about how maths can support inclusive public spaces (SDG 11).
justice_prompts: How might maths help us design inclusive and human spaces?
tools: D-ID: Chat with Fibonacci (link in lesson)

expressive_self.focus: Students notice ratios in everyday objects, express the golden ratio through metaphor, and design simple patterns that follow chosen ratios.
activities: Draw an object and label estimated ratios (e.g., width:height of a door).
Write a metaphor for the golden ratio (“It’s like…”).
Design a pattern or flag using a 2:3 or 3:5 ratio for colour or shapes.
Reflect: What ratios do you notice in your everyday surroundings?

designed_world.focus: Students explore the golden ratio inside a pentagram by measuring segments and comparing long:short ratios, then apply ratio thinking to a design task (poster/room/garden/building layout).
tools: Embedded interactive pentagram (Canvas file) to measure and compare ratios
activities: Measure segments in the pentagram and compare ratios.
Calculate long ÷ short — is it close to 1.618?
Identify other places you see this ratio in the world.
design_challenges: Create a poster, room, garden, or building layout using the golden ratio and explain how ratios influenced your choices.

regulation_supports: Move to a rhythm in 2:3 time — one beat for movement, one for breath.
Trace your hand or face and estimate ratios — can you find golden proportions?

pathways.name: Option A: Sustainable Public Space Ratio Plan (SDG 11)
pathways.focus: Design a sustainable public space using simple ratios (e.g., benches to trees) and justify your choices.
products: A design sketch/plan with at least two labelled ratios
A short explanation of how the ratios support sustainability and inclusion
At least one worked ratio calculation (simplifying or scaling)
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding: Simplifies and compares ratios accurately
Fluency & Accuracy: Solves part-to-whole and scaling problems
Application: Uses ratios meaningfully in a design context
Creativity & Reflection: Thoughtful and expressive design choices

pathways.name: Option B: Golden Ratio Artwork or Layout
pathways.focus: Create artwork or a layout using the golden ratio and explain how you verified proportions.
products: An artwork/layout using golden ratio proportions
Measurements showing long ÷ short is approximately 1.618 in at least one place
A short explanation of where the golden ratio appears in your design
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding: Explains the golden ratio as a proportional relationship
Fluency & Accuracy: Uses measurement and division to test ratios
Application: Connects golden ratio to design choices
Creativity & Reflection: Original and reflective presentation

pathways.name: Option C: Reflection on Ratios, Fairness, and Beauty
pathways.focus: Write a reflection on how understanding ratios supports fairness and beauty in the world.
products: A written reflection (or voice note) linking ratios to fairness and design
At least one mathematical example (simplified ratio or golden ratio test)
A short personal insight: where you notice ratios in everyday life
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding: Uses ratio language accurately
Application: Links ratio thinking to real-world design/fairness
Creativity & Reflection: Thoughtful personal voice and insight

required: Confident with factors and simplifying by dividing by a common factor

builds_toward: Geometric reasoning with proportionality in shapes
